4. System Loop
•Continuously monitors feeding schedule and commands.
•Logs all events for user tracking and alerts.
256.4 Prototype
fig.13 Prototype of Smart Pet Feeder
26Chapter 7. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE
SCOPE
7.1 CONCLUSION:
The Smart Pet Feeder system successfully demonstrates the potential of integrating IoT, auto-
mated dispensing mechanisms, sensors, and mobile-based controls into a modern, efficient, and
intelligent pet care solution. Through the use of microcontrollers, weight sensors, and pro-
grammable schedules, the system ensures accurate portion control, timely feeding, and reduces
human dependency for daily pet care.
The implementation of mobile app controls and notifications adds a significant layer of conve-
nience and remote management, making the system more user-friendly and accessible to pet
owners. Additionally, alerts for low food levels, dispenser jams, or irregular feeding behavior
ensure that pets are fed reliably, and owners are informed promptly regardless of their location.
Experimental results confirmed the system’s reliability, achieving precise portion control, accu-
rate feeding schedules, and consistent alert notifications under various conditions. The solution
is scalable and can be adapted for multiple pets, different types of food, and homes with varying
sizes, making it a practical step toward smarter and more autonomous pet care systems.
In conclusion, this project not only enhances traditional pet feeding routines but also sets
the foundation for future improvements such as AI-based feeding recommendations, behavior
monitoring, cloud analytics, multi-sensor integration, and seamless integration with broader
smart home ecosystems. These future enhancements will make pet care more intelligent, safe,
and convenient for both pets and their owners.
277.2 FUTURE SCOPE:
The current smart pet feeder system demonstrates an automated, IoT-enabled approach to pet
care by integrating sensors, mobile control, and scheduled feeding. However, there are several
areas where the system can be further enhanced in future developments:
1. AI-Based Feeding Recommendations
Implementing AI can help determine optimal feeding portions and times based on pet type, age,
weight, and activity level. This can prevent overfeeding or underfeeding and improve pet health.
2. Cloud-Based Data Storage and Analytics
Adding cloud storage can allow owners to track feeding history and monitor pet activity remotely.
Cloud AI could also analyze trends to provide insights, such as dietary adjustments or alerting
abnormal eating patterns.
3. Mobile App Enhancements
A dedicated mobile app can provide real-time notifications, remote feeding control, and alerts
for low food levels or dispenser jams. Users could also customize feeding schedules, portions,
and multiple pet profiles directly from their phones.
4. Edge AI Optimization
Future models can run AI algorithms locally on devices like Raspberry Pi or ESP32, reducing
reliance on constant internet connectivity and ensuring low-latency decision-making.
5. Multi-Sensor Integration
Adding sensors such as weight sensors, infrared, and cameras can increase accuracy in portion
control and detect food spillage or pet presence.
6. Behavior Monitoring and Alerts
Future systems could monitor pet behavior during feeding, detecting signs of health issues,
aggression, or anxiety, and alerting the owner proactively.
7. Voice Command Support
Integrating multi-language voice recognition can allow pet owners to feed or monitor their pets
using voice commands for convenience.
8. Smart Home Integration
Seamless integration with platforms like Google Home or Alexa could enable automated rou-
tines, such as feeding after a walk or exercise, or controlling lighting and environment for the pet.
289. Tamper Detection and Maintenance Alerts
Upgrades can detect dispenser tampering, food blockages, or low battery conditions, notifying
the user immediately to prevent feeding interruptions.
10. Health and Diet Compliance
Future versions can ensure dietary recommendations follow veterinary guidelines, track nutrient
intake, and maintain historical feeding data to comply with pet health standards.

title: PDF Memory - clound computing.pdf
description:
Experiment No.: 1 
Date: ___________ 
 
Title: 
Study the requirements of Ubuntu OS and create a Virtual Machine for installing Ubuntu on 
Windows. 
 
Aim: 
To study the system requirements of Ubuntu and create a Virtual Machine on Windows for 
installing Ubuntu OS. 
 
Theory: 
Ubuntu is an open-source Linux operating system based on Debian. It is widely used for learning 
Linux, programming, networking, and cybersecurity. 
To install Ubuntu on Windows without affecting the host system, virtualization software such as 
Oracle VM Virtual Box or VMware Workstation is used. 
A Virtual Machine (VM) allows multiple operating systems to run on a single computer. The 
main OS is called the Host OS (Windows), and the OS installed inside the VM is called the 
Guest OS (Ubuntu). 
 
System Requirements for Ubuntu 
Minimum Requirements: 
 Processor: 2 GHz dual-core processor 
 RAM: 2 GB 
 Storage: 25 GB free disk space 
 Display: 1024 × 768 resolution 
 Bootable USB / ISO file 
Recommended Requirements:  Processor: 2.5 GHz dual-core or higher 
 RAM: 4 GB or more 
 Storage: 30 GB or more 
 Internet connection (for updates) 
 
Software Requirements: 
1. Windows 7 / Windows 8 (Host OS) 
2. VirtualBox or VMware Workstation 
3. Ubuntu ISO file (downloaded from official website) 
 
Procedure 
 
Part A: Study Ubuntu Requirements 
1. Check system configuration of host PC: 
o Right click on This PC → Properties 
o Verify RAM, Processor, and available disk space 
2. Compare host configuration with Ubuntu requirements. 
3. Ensure system supports virtualization (Enable Virtualization from BIOS if required). 
 
Part B: Create Virtual Machine for Ubuntu Installation 
Step 1: Install VirtualBox 
1. Download VirtualBox. 
2. Run setup file. 
3. Click Next → Install → Finish. 
 
Step 2: Create New Virtual Machine 
1. Open VirtualBox. 
2. Click on New. 
3. Enter Name: Ubuntu 
4. Type: Linux 5. Version: Ubuntu (64-bit) 
6. Click Next. 
 
Step 3: Allocate Memory 
 Assign at least 2048 MB (2GB) 
 Recommended: 4096 MB (4GB) 
 
Step 4: Create Virtual Hard Disk 
1. Select “Create a virtual hard disk now”. 
2. Choose VDI format. 
3. Select Dynamically Allocated. 
4. Set size: Minimum 25GB. 
5. Click Create. 
 
Step 5: Attach Ubuntu ISO File 
1. Select the created VM. 
2. Click Settings → Storage. 
3. Under Controller IDE, select Empty. 
4. Click Disk icon → Choose Ubuntu ISO file. 
5. Click OK. 
 
Step 6: Start Installation 
1. Click Start. 
2. Select “Install Ubuntu”. 
3. Choose Language. 
4. Select Installation Type. 
5. Create username and password. 
6. Click Install. 
7. Restart after installation. 
 
Result: The system requirements of Ubuntu OS were studied and a virtual machine was successfully 
created on Windows for installing Ubuntu OS. 
 
Conclusion: 
Ubuntu can be installed safely using virtualization without affecting the host Windows OS. 
Virtual machines provide an isolated and secure environment for learning and experimentation. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Experiment No.: 2 
Date: ___________ 
 
Title: 
Procedure to create a Virtual Machine with Two CPU cores, 2GB RAM, and 20GB Storage and 
demonstrate installation of Ubuntu. 
 
Aim: 
To create a Virtual Machine with 2 CPU cores, 2GB RAM, and 20GB storage, and 
demonstrate installation of Ubuntu on the virtual machine. 
 
Theory: 
A Virtual Machine (VM) allows multiple operating systems to run on a single physical system 
using virtualization technology. The VM uses virtual hardware resources such as CPU, RAM, 
and hard disk that are allocated from the host system. 
Virtualization software like Oracle VM VirtualBox or VMware Workstation enables users to 
customize hardware configurations such as: 
 Number of CPU cores 
 Amount of RAM 
 Storage capacity 
These resources can be modified based on system requirements before installing the operating 
system. 
 
Software Requirements: 
 Windows 7 / 8 / 10 (Host OS) 
 VirtualBox / VMware Workstation 
 Ubuntu ISO File 
 Procedure 
 
Part A: Create Virtual Machine with Required Configuration 
Step 1: Open VirtualBox 
1. Launch VirtualBox. 
2. Click on New. 
 
Step 2: Configure Basic Details 
1. Enter Name: Ubuntu-VM 
2. Type: Linux 
3. Version: Ubuntu (64-bit) 
4. Click Next. 
 
Step 3: Allocate Memory 
 Set RAM to 2048 MB (2GB). 
 Click Next. 
 
Step 4: Create Virtual Hard Disk 
1. Select Create a virtual hard disk now. 
2. Choose VDI (VirtualBox Disk Image). 
3. Select Dynamically Allocated. 
4. Set disk size to 20 GB. 
5. Click Create. 
 
Step 5: Set CPU Cores 
1. Select the created VM. 
2. Click Settings → System → Processor. 
3. Increase processor count to 2 CPUs. 
4. Click OK.  
Part B: Install Ubuntu on the Virtual Machine 
Step 1: Attach Ubuntu ISO 
1. Select VM → Click Settings → Storage. 
2. Under Controller IDE, click Empty. 
3. Select Disk icon → Choose Ubuntu ISO file. 
4. Click OK. 
 
Step 2: Start Installation 
1. Click Start. 
2. Select Install Ubuntu. 
3. Choose Language. 
4. Select Installation Type (Erase disk and install Ubuntu). 
5. Create username and password. 
6. Click Install Now. 
7. Wait for installation to complete. 
8. Restart VM. 
 
Verification: 
After installation: 
1. Open Terminal in Ubuntu. 
2. Type the following commands to verify configuration: 
To check CPU: 
lscpu 
To check RAM: 
free -h 
To check storage: 
lsblk 
Confirm that:  CPU cores = 2 
 RAM = 2GB 
 Storage ≈ 20GB 
 
Result: 
A Virtual Machine was successfully created with 2 CPU cores, 2GB RAM, and 20GB storage, 
and Ubuntu OS was installed successfully. 
 
Conclusion: 
The experiment demonstrated how to allocate specific hardware resources (CPU, RAM, Storage) 
to a virtual machine and install Ubuntu successfully. Virtualization allows flexible and efficient 
resource management for testing and learning purposes. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Experiment No.: 3 
Date: ___________ 
 
Title: 
Install a C compiler in the Virtual Machine created using VirtualBox and execute a program. 
 
Aim: 
To install a C compiler in the virtual machine created using Oracle VM VirtualBox and execute a 
C program in Ubuntu. 
 
Theory: 
A C compiler is a software tool that converts C source code into machine-readable executable 
code. In Ubuntu Linux, the most commonly used C compiler is GCC (GNU Compiler 
Collection). 
GCC allows users to: 
 Compile C programs 
 Detect syntax errors 
 Generate executable files 
 Run programs in terminal 
Ubuntu provides GCC through the package manager using the build-essential package. 
 
Software Requirements: 
 Ubuntu installed in Virtual Machine 
 Internet connection 
 Terminal access 
 
Procedure  
Part A: Install C Compiler (GCC) 
Step 1: Open Terminal 
 Click on Applications → Terminal 
OR 
 Press Ctrl + Alt + T 
 
Step 2: Update Package List 
Type the following command: 
sudo apt update 
Enter password when prompted. 
 
Step 3: Install GCC Compiler 
Type: 
sudo apt install build-essential 
Press Y to confirm installation. 
 
Step 4: Verify GCC Installation 
Check version using: 
gcc --version 
If GCC version is displayed, installation is successful. 
 
 
 Part B: Create and Execute a C Program 
 
Step 1: Create a C Program File 
Type: 
nano program.c 
 
Step 2: Write the Following C Program 
#include <stdio.h> 
 
int main() { 
    printf("Hello, World!\n"); 
    return 0; 
} 
Save the file: 
 Press Ctrl + O → Enter 
 Press Ctrl + X 
 
Step 3: Compile the Program 
Type: 
gcc program.c -o program 
 
Step 4: Execute the Program 
Type: 
./program 
 
Output: 
Hello, World! 
 
 Result: 
The C compiler (GCC) was successfully installed in the Ubuntu virtual machine, and a C 
program was compiled and executed successfully. 
 
Conclusion: 
The experiment demonstrated the installation of a C compiler in a virtual environment and 
execution of a basic C program. This setup allows development and testing of C programs safely 
inside a virtual machine. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Experiment No.: 4 
Date: ___________ 
 
Title: 
Find a procedure to transfer files from one Virtual Machine to another Virtual Machine. 
 
Aim: 
To study and implement the procedure to transfer files from one Virtual Machine to another 
Virtual Machine. 
 
Theory: 
Virtual Machines (VMs) created using Oracle VM VirtualBox run as isolated systems. However, 
file transfer between VMs is possible using several methods such as: 
 Shared Folder Method 
 Network File Transfer (SSH / SCP) 
 USB Device Sharing 
 Cloud Storage 
In this experiment, we demonstrate the Shared Folder Method and Network (SCP) Method 
using Ubuntu virtual machines. 
 
Method 1: Using Shared Folder (Via Host System) 
Step 1: Create Shared Folder in Host OS 
1. Create a folder in Windows (Example: VM_Shared). 
2. Right-click → Properties → Sharing → Enable Sharing. 
 
 Step 2: Add Shared Folder in VirtualBox 
1. Select VM1 → Click Settings. 
2. Go to Shared Folders. 
3. Click Add New Folder. 
4. Select the created folder (VM_Shared). 
5. Enable: 
o Auto-mount 
o Make Permanent 
6. Click OK. 
Repeat the same steps for VM2. 
 
Step 3: Access Shared Folder in Ubuntu VM 
Open Terminal and type: 
sudo mount -t vboxsf VM_Shared /mnt 
Now: 
 Copy file from VM1 to shared folder. 
 Access same file from VM2 via shared folder. 
 
Method 2: Using Network (SCP Command) 
This method transfers files directly between two Ubuntu VMs. 
 
Step 1: Configure Network 
1. Go to VM Settings → Network. 
2. Select Bridged Adapter or Internal Network for both VMs. 
3. Start both VMs. 
 
 
 Step 2: Check IP Address 
In both VMs, type: 
ip a 
Note the IP addresses. 
 
Step 3: Install SSH Server (If not installed) 
sudo apt install openssh-server 
 
Step 4: Transfer File Using SCP 
From VM1: 
scp filename username@IP_Address:/home/username/ 
Example: 
scp file.txt user@192.168.1.10:/home/user/ 
Enter password when prompted. 
File will be transferred to VM2. 
 
Method 3: Using USB Device 
1. Insert USB device in host system. 
2. Go to VM Settings → USB. 
3. Enable USB Controller. 
4. Add USB device. 
5. Copy file from VM1 → USB → VM2. 
 
Result: 
Files were successfully transferred from one virtual machine to another using Shared Folder and 
Network (SCP) methods.  
Conclusion: 
File transfer between virtual machines can be achieved using multiple techniques such as shared 
folders, network transfer, or external devices. Network-based transfer is more efficient for large 
files, while shared folders are simpler for basic file exchange. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Experiment No.: 5 
Date: ___________ 
 
Title: 
Install Google App Engine. Create Hello World app and other simple web applications using 
Python. 
 
Aim: 
To install Google App Engine and develop a Hello World application and other simple web 
applications using Python. 
 
Theory: 
Google App Engine (GAE) is a Platform as a Service (PaaS) provided by Google Cloud. It 
allows developers to build and deploy web applications without managing servers. 
App Engine supports multiple programming languages including Python, Java, Node.js, and Go. 
It automatically handles scaling, load balancing, and deployment. 
 
Requirements: 
 Google App Engine 
 Internet Connection 
 Python (2.5 or higher) 
 Web Browser 
 
 
 Procedure 
 
Step 1: Download & Install Google App Engine 
                   Use following link to download Google App Engine 
                   https://google-app-engine.en.uptodown.com/windows/download 
                   Install the Google App Engine 
Step 2: Download & Install Python (2.5 or higher) 
              Download Python from python.org website 
                     Install python 
Step 3: Google App Engine Settings 
              Click Edit -> Click Preferences 
                    Set Python path :    C:\Python25\python.exe 
                     Set Google App Engine path :  C:\Program Files (x86)\Google\google_appengine 
Step 4: Create hello world Application 
                   Create new folder on Desktop named app 
                   Open Google App Engine -> Click on File -> create new application 
                   Give the name engineapp 
                   Set application path : C:\Users\Admin\Desktop\app , set port : 8080 
                   Click on Create-> project will be created in folder 
Step 5: Running the application 
                   Open Google App Engine -> Click on engineapp application  -> Run 
                   Open browser -> type  localhost:8080 - LocalHost.Co 
Result: Google App Engine was successfully installed, and a Hello World application along with another 
simple Python web application was created and deployed. 
 
Conclusion: 
The experiment demonstrated how to install and use Google App Engine for deploying Python-
based web applications. App Engine provides automatic scaling and managed infrastructure, 
making it suitable for cloud-based application development. 
 
 
 
                   
 
 
 
 
            
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Experiment No.: 6 
DATE: ____________ 
Title: 
Use Google App Engine (GAE) Launcher to launch the web applications. 
 
Aim: 
To use GAE Launcher to launch and deploy a web application on Google App Engine. 
 
Theory: 
Google App Engine (GAE) is a Platform as a Service (PaaS) provided by Google under Google 
Cloud Platform. 
It allows developers to: 
 Build web applications 
 Deploy applications on Google servers 
 Scale applications automatically 
 Manage hosting without worrying about infrastructure 
 
What is GAE Launcher? 
GAE Launcher is a tool used to: 
 Run applications locally 
 Test applications before deployment 
 Deploy applications to Google Cloud 
(Note: The old GAE Launcher has been replaced by Google Cloud SDK, but this experiment 
follows traditional lab procedure.) 
 
System Requirements:  Windows 10 / Linux 
 Python Installed 
 Google Cloud SDK Installed 
 Internet Connection 
 Google Account 
 
Prerequisites: 
1. Install Python (2.7 or 3.x) 
2. Install Google Cloud SDK 
3. Configure using: 
gcloud init 
 
Procedure: 
Step 1: Install Google Cloud SDK 
1. Download Google Cloud SDK. 
2. Install it. 
3. Open Command Prompt. 
4. Run: 
gcloud init 
5. Login using your Google account. 
 
Step 2: Create App Engine Project 
1. Go to Google Cloud Console. 
2. Create New Project. 
3. Enable App Engine. 
4. Select Region (e.g., asia-south1 for India). 
 
Step 3: Create Hello World Application 
Create a folder: hello-world 
Inside it create two files: 
1. main.py 
from flask import Flask 
app = Flask(__name__) 
 
@app.route('/') 
def hello(): 
    return "Hello, World!" 
 
if __name__ == '__main__': 
    app.run(host='127.0.0.1', port=8080, debug=True)

runtime: python39 
entrypoint: python main.py 
 
Step 4: Run Application Locally

python main.py

http://localhost:8080

Step 5: Deploy Application Using GAE Launcher / SDK

https://project-id.appspot.com

Thus, we successfully used Google App Engine Launcher to:

 Experiment No.: 7

Title:

 Experiment No.: 8 
Date: ____________ 
Title: 
Simulate a Cloud Scenario using CloudSim and Run a Scheduling Algorithm Not Present in 
CloudSim 
 
Aim: 
To simulate a cloud computing environment using CloudSim and implement a custom 
scheduling algorithm that is not available by default. 
 
Theory: 
CloudSim is a simulation toolkit used for modeling and simulating cloud computing 
environments. It allows researchers and students to test cloud resource provisioning policies 
without real cloud infrastructure. 
Default Scheduling Algorithms in CloudSim: 
 FCFS (First Come First Serve) 
 Round Robin 
 Time Shared 
 Space Shared 
In this experiment, we implement a Priority-Based Scheduling Algorithm, which is not 
available by default in basic CloudSim. 
 
Software Requirements: 
 Java JDK 
 NetBeans / Eclipse IDE 
 CloudSim library (cloudsim.jar) 
 
Algorithm Used: Priority-Based Scheduling Concept: 
 Each cloudlet (task) is assigned a priority value. 
 Higher priority cloudlets are executed first. 
 If two cloudlets have same priority, FCFS is applied. 
 
Procedure: 
Step 1: Setup Environment 
1. Install Java JDK. 
2. Install Eclipse or NetBeans. 
3. Download CloudSim library. 
4. Create a new Java project. 
5. Add cloudsim.jar to project libraries. 
 
Step 2: Create CloudSim Simulation Program 
Create a Java class named PrioritySchedulingExample.java. 
 
Step 3: Write Code to Initialize CloudSim 
CloudSim.init(num_user, calendar, trace_flag); 
 
Step 4: Create Datacenter 
Datacenter datacenter0 = createDatacenter("Datacenter_0"); 
 
Step 5: Create Broker 
DatacenterBroker broker = new DatacenterBroker("Broker"); 
 
Step 6: Create Virtual Machines (VMs) 
Vm vm = new Vm(vmId, brokerId, mips, pesNumber, ram, bw, size, vmm,  
               new CloudletSchedulerTimeShared());  
Step 7: Create Cloudlets with Priority 
Add a priority attribute: 
class PriorityCloudlet extends Cloudlet { 
    private int priority; 
 
    public PriorityCloudlet(..., int priority) { 
        super(...); 
        this.priority = priority; 
    } 
 
    public int getPriority() { 
        return priority; 
    } 
} 
 
Step 8: Sort Cloudlets by Priority 
Collections.sort(cloudletList, new Comparator<PriorityCloudlet>() { 
    public int compare(PriorityCloudlet c1, PriorityCloudlet c2) { 
        return c2.getPriority() - c1.getPriority(); 
    } 
}); 
 
Step 9: Submit Cloudlets 
broker.submitCloudletList(cloudletList); 
 
Step 10: Start Simulation 
CloudSim.startSimulation(); 
CloudSim.stopSimulation(); 
 
Expected Output: 
 Cloudlets with higher priority are executed first. 
 Execution start time of higher priority tasks will be earlier than lower priority tasks. 
 
Result: The cloud environment was successfully simulated using CloudSim. A custom Priority-Based 
Scheduling algorithm was implemented and executed successfully. 
 
Conclusion: 
CloudSim allows flexible implementation of new scheduling algorithms. The Priority-Based 
Scheduling algorithm improved task handling by executing high-priority tasks first.
